sections.heading: Why Most Goals Fail
sections.body: Every January, millions of developers set goals like 'learn a new framework' or 'get healthier.' By February, most have abandoned them. The problem isn't willpower — it's vagueness. Goals like 'be more productive' give your brain nothing concrete to work toward. SMART goals fix this by making every goal Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.

sections.heading: The SMART Framework
sections.body: SMART goals transform vague intentions into actionable commitments:

code.snippet:
SMART GOAL EXAMPLES:

❌ VAGUE: 'Learn TypeScript'
✅ SMART: 'Complete the TypeScript tutorial on official docs and build
          one small project using TypeScript by March 15'

❌ VAGUE: 'Get better at system design'
✅ SMART: 'Read "Designing Data-Intensive Applications" and write a
          summary of each chapter, completing 1 chapter per week
          for 12 weeks'

❌ VAGUE: 'Exercise more'
✅ SMART: 'Run 3 times per week (Mon/Wed/Fri) for 30 minutes
          before work, tracked in Strava, for the next 8 weeks'

THE SMART CHECKLIST:
S - Specific: What exactly will you do?
M - Measurable: How will you know when it's done?
A - Achievable: Is this realistic given your constraints?
R - Relevant: Does this align with your career/life goals?
T - Time-bound: What's the deadline?
code.explanation: Notice how each SMART goal answers WHO, WHAT, WHEN, and HOW. If you can't answer these, the goal is too vague.

sections.heading: The 1-3-5 Rule
sections.body: Don't set 20 goals — you'll achieve none. Use the 1-3-5 rule: 1 big goal, 3 medium goals, 5 small goals. The big goal is your 'mountain top' — where you want to be in 6-12 months. Medium goals are quarterly milestones. Small goals are weekly actions that move you toward the medium ones. This creates a clear line of sight from today's actions to your long-term vision.

jargon.definition: A goal-setting framework: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Makes vague intentions into concrete commitments.
A prioritization method: 1 big goal, 3 medium goals, 5 small goals. Prevents goal overload.
What you want to achieve (e.g., 'get promoted to senior'). Focus on this, but control it through process goals.
What you do daily/weekly to reach the outcome (e.g., 'code 2 hours daily on the promotion project'). You fully control this.

summary: SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) transform vague intentions into actionable commitments. Use the 1-3-5 rule: 1 big goal, 3 medium goals, 5 small goals. Focus on process goals (daily actions) rather than just outcome goals (desired results) — you control the process, not the outcome.
